story:
Christmas, to me, is a jumble of flavors, sounds, and colors: pigs in blankets and sleigh bells, a weather balloon strung with colored lights marking the way to our house. Mama paints our big picture window with a jaunty winter panorama—crazy snowmen and happy children bundled in bright coats and hats—proof that a real artist lives here. Dad adds his own magic with giant green plywood pine trees, flashing red and green lights, and shiny aluminum stars standing guard by our humble front door. But my favorite memory is of the Brownies.

I saw them. I truly did. Not those mall “elves,” but Brownies—miniature, sneaky, tricky, and fast. They appear the day after Thanksgiving—B-Day, Brownie Day—when we children shift into high-alert goodness. Teasing my little sister is out; cheerful dish duty is in. The Brownies are everything at once: reward, bribe, warning, and witness. If I’m good, they’ll tell Santa. If I’m even a little bad, they’ll tell Santa. They see everything. No wonder I’m a sugar-fueled, secret-keeping wreck by Christmas Day.

The day I spotted them is burned into my six-year-old memory. A clear, bright afternoon. I was gazing out our window at the sugar beet field across the street, its frozen ground tucked under a thick, sparkling layer of pristine snow. By the empty irrigation canal, an old wooden boat lay upside down like a sleeping whale. Then—movement. Just a flicker. Not a cat. Not a dog. Not one of Stoddard’s runaway rabbits. A Brownie. Of course it was. So that’s where they hid, those tiny spies I could never catch. A plan unspooled in my head.

After weeks of fretting over where to leave my Christmas letter, I knew the perfect place: under the boat, where the Brownies would be sure to find it. Only one problem—I wasn’t allowed to cross the street alone. So I told my dad. He understood, as only a former kid can.

At dusk, hand in hand, we crossed the street and crunched through the frosty stillness of the field. My letter was warm in my mittened fist; my legs tingled with excitement and nerves. Along the ditch bank we went. What if I actually saw a Brownie? Would I drop the note and sprint home? I scanned the snow for tiny footprints, for any nibble of proof. I felt watched—in the best, scariest way. Would they think I was clever, finding their hideout? Would they nudge Santa and say, This girl means business?

I’ll never know what they thought of my daring delivery. I do know that when Dad walked me back the next morning, the letter was gone. All that remained were a few cookie crumbs and candy wrappers, as if there’d been a secret meeting with refreshments. And somehow, Santa knew my wish list perfectly.

Even now, I’m convinced the Brownies are real. At Christmastime, thinking of them makes me want to be good, do good—be the kind of kid someone might be rooting for. It’s nice to feel watched over, cheered on. Maybe I should think of them all year long. What could it hurt?
